chapters.title: How nationalism slides into fascism
chapters.text:
Today’s political spectrum is wide and varied. New ideas and parties keep appearing, but one thing stays the same, the split between the right and the left. Right-wing leaders preach the basics of nationalism, a strong love and devotion to the country. It is a noble and dignified feeling, and patriots claim to have the state’s best interests at heart. Left unchecked, though, nationalism can grow into something extreme and dangerous, and that is fascism.

Fascism is not a relic of the past, as some would like to think. It thrived in the twentieth century when Germany and Italy made it the central doctrine during the Second World War. Yet elements that mirror fascist ideology are still around in modern political systems. The symptoms include propaganda, an unhealthy fixation on the nation’s heroic past, a strict social hierarchy, and more. With these tools, the leader can plant ideas of supremacy into the minds of citizens.

Fascists claim that the nation’s superiority can justify harsh treatment of other groups in society.

Many present-day countries have stepped onto the path of nationalism. Far-right parties dominate Poland, Hungary, Turkey, and Russia. In the United States, the trend became obvious when Donald Trump started his run for the presidency. He is a firm backer of anti-immigrant policy and keeps attacking refugees from the Arab region who flee to the US. That hostility has led to restrictions and bans on people looking for a better life in America and trying to escape the horrors of war. This summary will cover the typical features of fascist dogma that are still alive in the twenty-first century political world.

chapters.title: Nostalgia for a mythic past fuels fascism
chapters.text:
At the core of fascist ideology sits a fetish for the nation’s past. It is the obsession with historic victories, military triumphs, and golden periods of prosperity. Fascist supporters want to stir up nostalgia for those glorious days and the urge to reclaim a purity that globalization and unconventional beliefs have spoiled. The nation’s wrongdoings stay behind the curtain. To bring up these pages of history is to damage the great state’s image. The restrictions even exist on the legal level. In Turkey, for instance, it is against the law to bring up the genocide of Armenians carried out by the Turkish nation during the First World War.

In the old days, society clung to the traditional family model, with the husband as the leader and the wife obedient to him. It is a perfect prototype for fascist politicians, who project this pattern onto the community. The head of state, whether a president or a party leader, is the father of the nation, and is to be revered and worshiped. That patriarchal influence gives the ruler of the country unlimited power and the ability to manipulate the masses. The nation itself hands the authority to the fascist leader, since people vote for this candidate in a democratic election. Democracy, however, ends right there, and tyranny and despotism take over.

The main tool of fascism is propaganda. The information that fascist supporters usually spread carries a noble ring and pulls people in. It can be calls to fight corruption or inequality, to restore law and justice, or to protect the country from dangerous enemies. The community rarely sees that behind this propaganda are dark goals of seizing power. The basis of the indoctrination is twisted facts, lies, and manipulation. The hypocrisy is clear. By hiding behind anti-corruption slogans and campaigns, the fascists run financial and political schemes of their own. They fight against people and institutions that do not share their vision or that defend unconventional ideas.

In fascist belief, any deviation from tradition or norm counts as corruption.

Women in Nazi Germany had to endure extreme misogyny because the common belief was that their only role was to raise children.

chapters.title: Fascist hostility to intellect and truth
chapters.text:
Schools and universities have always been a threat to fascist ideology. They act as independent bodies with freedom of speech and a range of opinions. That wide spread of views undermines the goal of politicians to gather the nation under one undisputed dogma. From a fascist point of view, schools and colleges should worship the country and its achievements and build pupils’ devotion to the state, instead of offering subjects that could grow critical thinking and tolerance. Gender studies are the evil of university programs, and the push for women’s equality was, in their telling, a plot by Jews. This kind of talk was common among Nazi supporters.

In the fight against intellectualism, fascists villainize and discredit educational institutions. In 2006, David Horowitz, a far-right advocate, released the work 101 Most Dangerous Professors in America. In that piece, he attacked liberal and left-leaning scholars who also stood up for the rights of Palestinians. The point of the statement was to accuse universities of bias against right-wing supporters and of killing free speech. Under that cover, Horowitz tried hard to spread far-right ideas.

The secret weapon of fascist politics is the conspiracy theory. It carries alarming claims about certain groups of people or a figure that supposedly prove their wicked intentions toward the nation. Conspiracy theories ran wild in Nazi Germany, where fascists tried to demonize the Jewish community. The prime example is The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, a supposed handbook for Jews on taking over the world. This conspiracy was a perfect excuse for the Nazi leaders to persecute Jews. Very few people today would buy those claims, since conspiracy theories are absurd. Even so, society’s grip on reality can wobble.

Conspiracy theories push people to doubt the media and newspapers and live in a constant state of paranoia.

In the eyes of far-right activists, the media that refuses to broadcast irrational conspiracy theories is biased and untrustworthy. In 2012, Donald Trump blasted CNN for not giving air to birtherism. The claim was that President Barack Obama was born not in the US but in Kenya, which made him unfit for office. Trump’s attacks were a clear attempt to weaken the press and plant doubt in people’s minds.

chapters.title: How fascists invent public enemies
chapters.text:
Fascist politicians actively reject liberal democracy and its principle of equality. The community is set up to follow a strict hierarchy, with men above women and the dominant nation at the top of the social pyramid. Nature supposedly defines this arrangement, since stronger individuals always managed to survive and hold more influence. A clear hierarchy gives those in charge levers of huge power.

The fascist hierarchy sets up a line between people. Hard-working, diligent citizens are entitled to the privileges, while on the other side sit passive individuals with no right to welfare benefits. In the US, some politicians use this story to separate the white population from the Black, leaning on the false idea that African Americans are prone to laziness.

Polls run among Trump’s supporters show an interesting pattern. Almost half of the respondents believed that the white population of the US had to endure the most discrimination. The same pattern showed up with religion, where 54% of those polled were sure Christians faced extreme oppression. These numbers show how the subtle tactic of victimization works. The top priority of far-right politicians is to present minorities as a threat to society and make dominant groups fear for their position. The strategy may sound absurd, yet it works. Every time minority representation in any field grows, so does the public support for right-wing parties and their anti-immigrant projects.

The fascist leader uses a sense of collective victimhood to build a group identity that is by its nature opposed to the cosmopolitan spirit and individualism of liberal democracy, as Jason Stanley once wrote.

Viktor Orban, Hungary’s prime minister, casts himself as a fierce defender of Christianity in Europe. According to Orban, this religion is under threat, and the reason is the huge wave of refugees and immigrants who, he says, aim to take over Europe with their beliefs. By giving speeches like that, Orban stirs up fear and anxiety across the nation.

Victimization helps build the illusion of us and them, the feeling of being surrounded by enemies, terrorists, and traitors.

chapters.title: Equality is the enemy fascists fear
chapters.text:
Just as fascists draw a line between privileged and non-privileged groups, the same split shows up in law and order. By default, the majority follows the rules, and the minority carries every trait of a criminal mind. Any departure from the fascist ideal, whether homosexuality, feminism, or liberalism, can be treated as a crime. The conflict comes from how the actions of the two groups are read. If a member of the dominant class commits an offense, they made a mistake and deserve a second chance. The opposite holds for a member of the minority, where even the smallest misstep is treated as a felony.

The media and broadcasting platforms can mirror the view of minority groups. The protests by the Black community in response to Freddie Gray’s death in police custody were labeled riots. It is no surprise that the media using that word was far-right. The choice of words adds to the negative image of African American people.

Fascists feel most exposed when the power of the father figure is at risk. The threat can come from interracial marriages, same-sex relationships, and transgender communities, all of which undermine the patriarchy and pollute the blood. To stop these ideas from spreading, fascists create myths about mass rapes and assaults to push sexual anxiety into the public mind. Look at the Russian propaganda machine. Not long ago, its outlets ran stories that Arab immigrants had tried to rape white women in Europe. The brutality story sparked a wave of protests from the Russian diaspora in Germany, a huge reaction to fabricated information.

By embracing our own femaleness and femininity, we cast a shadow of doubt over the supposed supremacy of maleness and masculinity, as Julia Serano once wrote.

The culture of equality hits the idea of hierarchy hard. Men expect to be honored and adored, since they are the breadwinners and family protectors. They believe they have the right to hold the reins. When women push back and show they can do the same things, they can face extreme misogyny and discrimination. Far-right activists hold the deepest hatred for the transgender community, especially trans women.

In the view of conservatives, it is unthinkable to step down from the dominant position of a male to a trans female.

chapters.title: Labor as the measure of a person’s worth
chapters.text:
Fascist politicians always set cosmopolitan cities against rural areas. Cities are the source of crime, vice, and sexual perversion. It is almost impossible to find the real spirit of the nation in urban areas, which are full of foreigners, immigrants, and refugees. On the other hand, the village is full of moral virtue and innocence. Rural residents stand in for the state and form the purest part of the population.

Far-right activists push the idea that villagers provide for city dwellers. They do the hard work while city people enjoy the results. In the twentieth century, this story pulled many villagers to the side of Nazism.

The notion of hard work is a key part of fascist philosophy. Only hard labor can ennoble people. Those who avoid it or look for an easy job are inferior and deserve condemnation. The Jewish community was more involved in intellectual work than in manual labor. That pattern drew fierce criticism and hatred from the fascists of Germany. Jews were labeled parasites and treated cruelly. German fascism, with its fixation on hard work, went so far that even people with disabilities were in danger, since they were subjected to forced sterilization and gassing. Later, doctors were ordered to kill such individuals.

The foundation of the perfect fascist community is a strong and healthy nation committed to work.

Far-right activists spread more than myths about certain minorities to discredit them. They also create the conditions that turn those stereotypes into reality. In Myanmar, the Rohingya ethnic group faced terrible oppression that rose to mass killings. It was the response to the sexual abuse and killing of a Buddhist woman by members of the Rohingya group. That event triggered many restrictions on the minority. They could not move freely around the country, and many lost their jobs. These conditions lowered the quality of life for the Rohingya, and the majority began to see them as outcasts and criminals.

The biased attitude of American authorities toward the Black community is clear. Because of the stereotypes and prejudice that Black people are more likely to commit crimes, they face a higher chance of imprisonment. A prison record hurts a work resume, and many African Americans face unemployment, which is a direct result of prejudice and favoritism.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Modern society sees fascism as something horrible and inhuman. People link it to the tragedies of the World Wars, when the ideology was on the rise. Fascism contributed to some of the darkest pages of history, the genocide of the Jewish people, racial and political oppression, and the degrading treatment of women. Humanity has learned the lesson the hard way, but what are the chances it will not repeat itself? History is cyclic, and the question is how to spot the birth of the next fascist movement. Far-right parties are not a small fringe. They keep winning popularity among voters in leading countries.

At the core of fascist politics is not the goal of helping the community or improving the state’s welfare. The main motive is to grab power and turn the nation into a voiceless crowd. Fascists use proven methods of brainwashing, including propaganda, victimization, and conspiracy theories. They aim to spread fear and resentment. The tentacles of fascist ideology are almost invisible, and society can read the first signs as harmless statements. A clear example is anti-immigrant policy, which to a casual eye may not look like a threat but is deeply xenophobic. The normalization of these narratives from politicians around the world can have serious fallout. Instead of spreading rumors, myths, and prejudice, leaders should promote empathy and open-mindedness.

A few things you can try. Work to remove stereotypical thinking and build an inner sense of tolerance for any race, sexuality, or gender. No group is less deserving of human rights and privileges. People are equal. Always try to think critically and weigh a situation from more than one angle.

Thanks for reading to the end.
